description: US-Israel military action began; subsequent shipping risk produced a de facto Hormuz closure
notes: EIA identifies February 28 as the start of military action and reports February Brent averaging about $71/bbl.

description: Major shipping companies halted Hormuz transit and Platts began reviewing Persian Gulf crude deliverability
notes: Article-level Platts source; Dated Brent was assessed at $70.94/bbl on February 27.

description: Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz sharply reduced commercial traffic
notes: Primary reference point. EIA dates the closure to March 2; Platts separately assessed Dated Brent at $77.815/bbl.

description: Hormuz oil flows remained near a standstill with seven vessel transits on March 3 versus 91 on February 28
notes: Article-level S&P Global evidence; price retained only as an approximate replay feature.

description: Hormuz traffic fell to three ships on March 9
notes: Article-level S&P Global Commodities at Sea report; price is an approximate replay feature.

description: IEA members agreed to release 400 million barrels from emergency reserves
notes: Primary IEA release; export volumes were reported below 10% of pre-conflict levels.

description: Brent crude surpassed $100 per barrel following the de facto closure
notes: EIA states Brent surpassed $100/bbl on March 12; value is a documented lower bound.

description: Middle East-to-Asia crude tanker rates reached their highest level since records began in 2005
notes: EIA article-level source; price is an approximate replay feature and not used as exact ground truth.

description: EIA reported March Brent averaging $103/bbl and forecast a second-quarter peak near $115/bbl
The United States and Iran agreed to a framework intended to reopen the Strait
notes: Article-level report; reopening was prospective and not treated as confirmed physical normalization.
